citeinfo.title: Characterization of water residence time, nutrients, phytoplankton and related water quality constituents in the Cache Slough Complex of the California Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta in 2017 and 2018

descript.abstract: Data was collected in the Cache Slough Complex located in the northern San Francisco Estuary to characterize water quality parameters at high spatial resolution. Data collection was conducted on three separate occasions: October 2017, May 2018, and October 2018. Data set includes nitrate, ammonium, ortho-phosphate, dissolved organic carbon, temperature, conductivity, dissolved oxygen, turbidity, chlorophyll, stable isotopes including water isotopes. Measurements were used to calculate evaporation to inflow ratios and water residence time.
descript.purpose: The dataset documents spatial and temporal variability of nutrients, related water quality parameters, and water residence time at high spatial resolution in the Delta. The dataset includes nutrients (N,P), temperature, conductivity, dissolved oxygen, chlorophyll, and water isotopes (δ2H, δ18O). Evaporation to inflow ratios and water residence times were calculated using the water isotope data. Data was collected on three separate mapping surveys: October 2017, May 2018, and October 2018.
descript.supplinf: See files: Corrections and Offsets; Data Dictionary for further information regarding the datasets.

idinfo.accconst: None. Please see 'Distribution Info' for details.
idinfo.useconst: Although this data set has been used by the U.S. Geological Survey, U.S. Department of the Interior, no warranty expressed or implied is made by the U.S. Geological Survey as to the accuracy of the data and related materials. The act of distribution shall not constitute any such warranty, and no responsibility is assumed by the U.S. Geological Survey in the use of this data, software, or related materials. Any use of trade, product, or firm names is for descriptive purposes only and does not imply endorsement by the U.S. Government.

attracc.attraccr: EXO sondes were calibrated before use in the field with the KorEXO software. For calibration, the sonde sensors are submerged one at a time in standards, then the verified chemical concentration is recorded within the instrument. If the instrument measures an unknown value of the parameter, it refers to the calibration information to calculate the concentration of the chemical in the water (EXO User Manual, pg. 81). See process step 4 for additional information.

dataqual.logic: Redundancy is built into the system, meaning many of the parameters included in the data set are measured by 2 different instruments. Data was displayed in real-time for on the spot user verification. Topology rules were applied to points, lines, and polygons to ensure the integrity of the geospatial data.
dataqual.complete: Data set is considered complete for the information presented, as described in the abstract. Users are advised to read the rest of the metadata record carefully for additional details.

horizpa.horizpar: We conducted a visual check that the GPS data we recorded matched the boat GPS. Data was also displayed in real-time, allowing us to spot check GPS data underway. In addition, GPS location information was compared to field notes that described known locations and features.

vertacc.vertaccr: We conducted a visual check that the GPS data we recorded matched the boat GPS. Data was also displayed in real-time, allowing us to spot check GPS data underway. In addition, GPS location information was compared to field notes that described known locations and features. Datum used NAD1983.

procstep.procdesc:
For analysis, sample water was continuously pumped onto the boat while underway at speeds up to 30 mph (13 m/s) using a pick-up tube mounted at a fixed depth of approximately 1 m below the surface, routed through a screen to remove large debris and into a pressure-compensated manifold that maintained system pressure at a proscribed level irrespective of boat speed (Downing et al., 2016). A 2-stage debubbler was used to remove bubbles that can interfere with optical measurements. Sample water was split into separate flow paths for each analytical flow path, with constant flow rates maintained by metering the discharge line, except for the flow path serving the open split interface, which was metered on the feed line. Flows were continuously monitored using a sight gauge for each flow path.
The manifold delivered water to the following flowpaths: (1) A flow-through system consisting of a thermosalinograph that recorded temperature and conductance (Sea-Bird Scientific SB45 (TSG), Belluve, WA); fluorometers that measured fluorescence of chlorophyll-a (fCHLA) and fluorescence of dissolved organic matter (fDOM, WETLabs WETstar (WS), Philomath, OR); a beam transmissometer that recorded transmittance and attenuation (WETLabs model C-Star transmissometer (CStar), Philomath, OR), a nitrate (NO3) analyzer (SUNA V2; Sea-Bird Scientific, Bellevue, WA). (2) A flow chamber for a multiparameter water quality sonde (YSI EXO2; Xylem Inc. (EXO), Rye Brook, New York) equipped with sensors to measure temperature, specific conductance (SpC), turbidity, pH, dissolved oxygen (DO), fDOM and fCHLA that then passed water to a fluorometer designed to measure different algal classes (FluoroProbe III (FP); BBE Moldaenke, Kiel, Germany). (3) An open-split interface at atmospheric pressure that served water filtered through a 0.45 micron high-capacity in-line groundwater sampling capsule filter (PALL Corporation, Port Washington, New York) to the on-board ammonium (NH4) analyzer water isotope (δ2H, δ18O) analyzer.
The NH4 analyzer was a continuous flow, gas diffusion/conductivity-based (Carlson 1986) analyzer for ammonium (NH4) analysis (TL-2800; Timberline Instruments, Boulder, CO) that was modified for field operation and continuous data collection by the manufacturer. Modifications included installation in a ruggedized housing, addition of an automated line-switching valve, addition of a heating unit to maintain the instrument at a constant above-ambient temperature, and changes to the software. Full standard curves were run at the beginning and end of each day and partial curves run throughout the day each time the boat stopped to sample. While underway, the analyzer was run in continuous mode with frequent periodic introduction of standard and blank solutions. The NH4 analyzer was connected to a stand-alone computer and collected data through its native software.
The water isotope analyzer employs cavity ring down spectroscopy to measure isotopic concentrations of δ2H and δ18O in real-time (Picarro Inc., Sunnyvale, CA). The instrument is a continuous flow instrument comprised of 2 separate instruments. The continuous water analyzer (CWS) pumps sample water from the open-split interface and vaporizes samples water across a membrane. The CWS delivers vaporized water to the CRDS where water isotope concentrations are measured and reported in real-time (Downing et al. 2011). Tertiary standards (Kona water) are measured approximately every 4 hours and are used to track instrument drift throughout the day. The instrument has an onboard computer where data is collected and stored through its native software.
All instrumentation was cleaned, and calibrations were checked prior to each use following the manufacturer's recommendation or as described below. Data for most instruments was recorded at 1-second frequency on a single data logger (CR6, Campbell Scientific, Logan, Utah, United States) together with a timestamp and boat position obtained from a high-resolution global positioning system receiver (16X-HVS, Garmin, Olathe, Kansas). The FluoroProbe logged data internally and to the host software. All data were displayed in real time, so scientists on board could respond when they observed changes relevant to study objectives, or if issues with flow or instrument performance were noted.
Discrete samples for determination of NH4, NO3, NO2, dissolved organic carbon (DOC), total dissolved nitrogen (TDN) concentrations, absorbance, fluorescense, stable isotopes, algal pigments, as well as phytoplankton enumeration and picocyanobacteria counts were collected, processed, and analyzed following traditional methods as described below, and used to validate and calibrate on-board instrumentation.

Discrete water samples were collected either underway or–in the case of more extensive sample requirements–while stopped on station. Discrete samples were collected while underway either using a fourth flow path on the sampling manifold or using a separate system with a separate pick-up tube mounted adjacent to the main sampling pick-up and pumping sample through a 0.45-micron filter into a sample bottle. Samples were collected while underway approximately every 2 miles, principally for determination of ortho-phosphate (PO4). At designated sampling stations (approximately 10 per day) samples for determination of NH4, NO3, PO4, dissolved organic carbon (DOC), and total dissolved nitrogen (TDN) concentrations were collected using this system. Additionally, unfiltered samples were collected approximately from 1 m depth using a submersible pump for analysis of chlorophyll-a (Chl-a), pheophytin, phycocyanin, allophycocyanin, and phycoerythrin pigments, phytoenumeration, and picocyanobacterial counts, and water isotopes. Samples for phytoplankton enumeration and picocyanobacteria counts were fixed immediately with Lugol’s iodine solution (4 mL) and formaldehyde (1 mL), respectively. Sample splits for accessory pigment analysis were collected by USGS but processed and analyzed by California State University Maritime Academy.

Laboratory Analytical Methods
Nutrients: Concentrations of nutrients were determined colorimetrically using an automated-segmented flow analyzer. Concentrations of nitrogen as nitrite (NO2-N) and as nitrate plus nitrite (NO3-N + NO2-N) were determined by colorimetric analysis (Fishman, 1993; Patton and Kryskalla, 2011), ammonium as nitrogen (NH4-N) was determined by colorimetric analysis after reaction with salicylate-hypochlorite (Fishman, 1993), and total dissolved nitrogen (TDN) was determined by alkaline persulfate digestion (Patton and Kryskalla 2003). Orthophosphate as phosphorous (PO4-P; also referred to as soluble reactive phosphate) was determined by colorimetric analysis after reaction with NH4 molybdate and reduction with ascorbic acid (Patton and Truitt, 1992).
Chlorophyll-a and phaeophytin: Chlorophyll-a and phaeophytin concentrations were determined according to EPA Method 445.0 (Arar and Collins, 1997) using sonication extraction techniques described by Jeffrey and others (1997) on a Turner TD-700 Genesis 10S (Turner Designs Inc., Sunnyvale, CA). Accessory pigment samples were processed by HPLIC (High Performance Liquid Chromatograph, Agilent) at the Horn Point Analytical Laboratory at the University of Maryland, following the protocols of Van Heukelem and Thomas (2001). The HLPC diagnostic pigments are chlorophyll-b for the chlorophytes (green algae), fucoxanthin (brown pigment diagnostic of the diatoms), alloxanthin for the cryptophytes, zeaxanthin for the cyanobacteria and peridinin for the dinoflagellates.
Phycocyanin, Allophycocyanin, and Phycoerythrin: Phycocyanin, allophycocyanin, and phycoerythrin were determined by azolectin-CHAPS buffer extraction as described by (Zimba, 2012) and modified from (Bennett and Bogorad, 1973) at BSA Environmental Services. Phytoplankton enumeration was completed in accordance with the American Public Health Association (APHA) Standard Method 10200 (2012).
Picocyanobacteria Direct Counts: Picocyanobacteria direct counts were enumerated via epifluorescence microscopy as described by (Murrell and Lores, 2004). Phycoerythrin-containing cells (PE) and phycocyanin-containing cells (PC) were counted separately and then summed to obtain total picocyanobacteria densities for cells sized between 0.2 and 2.0 microns.
DOC: Dissolved organic carbon concentrations were measured using a total organic carbon analyzer (TOC-VCSH, Shimadzu Scientific Instruments, Columbia, Maryland) using high-temperature catalytic combustion according to a modified version of USEPA method 415.3 (Potter and Wimsatt, 2005).
Absorbance spectra and fluorescence EEMs were simultaneously measured using an Aqualog® equipped with a photodiode and charge-coupled device (CCD) (Horiba Instruments, NJ, USA) according to the method described by Hansen et al. (2018). Briefly, excitation and absorbance spectra were measured using a 150 W xenon lamp, a 5 nm bandpass, and a 1 s integration time at wavelengths of 240–600 nm. Emission spectra were collected with a CCD at approximately 1.64 nm (4 pixel) intervals at wavelengths of 250–600 nm. To reduce ultraviolet light exposure of the sample and limit the effects of photobleaching, excitation and absorbance wavelengths were scanned from low to high energy (i.e., visible to ultraviolet range) during analysis. Peaks, ratios, and slopes reported in the data were calculated as described by Hansen et al. (2016).
Stable Isotopes: δ 2H and δ 18O of H2O: Sample were analyzed at USGS Reston Stable Isotope Lab using dual-inlet isotope ratio mass spectrometry according to the methods of Révész and Coplen (2008). δ 13C, δ 15N, and C:N ratio of Particulate Organic Matter: Particulate organic matter was collected via filtration through 0.7 μm pre-combusted glass fiber filters, prepared, and analyzed at MPSIL following the method described in Kendall et al. (2001), using an Optima mass spectrometer. Samples are weighed in silver boats and then vapor acidified to remove any carbonate prior to analysis. C:N values are reported as atomic (at) ratios. δ

Data Processing
Data from the Fluoroprobe (FP) was integrated with the other instrument data using the timestamp. Where necessary, instrument-specific scale factors and corrections were applied to the time series data using pandas software library in Python. This script was also used to remove periods of compromised data (due to flow blockages, bubbles, or for some other reason), and to apply a centered 20 second median filter to the time series.
Nitrate Data Processing: The SUNA nitrate analyzer measures NO3 concentrations spectrophotometrically. We calibrated the instrument response against the measured concentrations in discrete samples collected over the course of the day. Correction to laboratory-measured NO3 values was accomplished for each day by regressing the laboratory data with the corresponding field data. Individual sample results with more than three standard deviations from the regression were judged to be outliers and removed from the regression. The resulting regression model was then applied to the high frequency time series data.
Ammonium Data Processing: The output voltage (V) data obtained from the Timberline NH4 analyzer was processed using Visual Basic script in Excel to correct for baseline drift and calibrate instrument response to the standards run throughout the day. The data were first corrected for baseline drift using the instrument response during periodic measurements of OFW (n ≥10) to define the baseline response. The V response for each of the standards in the calibration curve was then calculated as the V deviation from the baseline, and a linear calibration curve was obtained by regression of the V responses to the standard concentrations. Calibrations and baseline offsets were reviewed statistically and graphically before final data approval. A regression coefficient of >0.97 R² was judged to be acceptable.
The microfluidic processing in the Timberline analyzer takes approximately 90 seconds, therefore the time series data from the Timberline must be lagged to the proper sample intake time of the remaining instrumentation. Thus, the NH4 time-series from this first level of processing was further modified by correcting the time value to obtain the best possible correspondence with other data sources, by visually maximizing alignment between data sets during periods of rapid change. The time lag-corrected NH4 data was then merged with time series data without additional processing.
Picarro water isotope Data Processing: Data was time lag-corrected to account for the time it takes the Picarro to intake and analyze sample water compared to flow through instrumentation. We calibrated the instrument response against the measured concentrations in discrete samples collected over the course of the day. Correction to laboratory-measured water isotope values was accomplished for each day by regressing the laboratory data with the corresponding field data. Individual sample results with more than three standard deviations from the regression were judged to be outliers and removed from the regression. The resulting regression model was then applied to the high frequency time series data. Lab calibrated water isotope data was then used to calculate evaporation to inflow ratios and water residence time (Downing et al. 2016).

Spatial Alignment (SA)
Data was spatially aligned (SA) to a common spatial framework to facilitate comparisons between dates and to minimize the effects of differential data density on the spatial interpolation calculations. The alignment was accomplished by assigning all data to a centerline vector comprising points located at the centerline of all channels navigated and spaced at approximately 150 m intervals.
The centerline vector was created to provide a single representative path through most of the Delta channels in a Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta polygon. This centerline vector created uniform spatial points across the Delta, facilitating temporal calculations with the same spatial reference. The centerline was created using the Production Centerline tool from ArcMap 10.6.1 Production Mapping toolbox. The Generate Points Along Line tool from the Data Management toolbox was used to produce equally distant points approximately every 150 m on the centerline vector, and each point was assigned a centerline identification number (CL ID). For each CL ID, a surrounding geographic extent was created using North and South latitudes and East and West longitudes to delineate geographic boundaries across which data would be integrated. A Visual Basic script in Microsoft Excel was then used to associate each sampling point data to the closest representative centerline point. The median value for each parameter was then calculated for all data points located within each extent and assigned to the CL ID. Centerline points in the domain of interest that were not associated with any measured values were assigned a value using the interpolation approach described below.
Interpolated water quality maps were created from the spatially aligned data using ArcGIS Spline with Barriers tool, which performs the minimum curvature method to minimize overall surface curvature. The tool calculated and compared the weighted summation from 12 neighboring cells to the target cell in the center. The method is in accordance with Terzopoulos (1988) and it is known as molecular summation. Spline with Barriers is part of ArcGIS Pro 2.3.0 Spatial Analyst toolbox and has an optional barrier input. In this case, the barrier was a Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta polygon obtained from the National Hydrography Dataset (NHD) and edited using ArcGIS Editor toolbar. The raster output was smoothed with the Focal Statistics tool from Data Management, and the mean method was applied on a 4x4 cell. Results were converted to vector format with the Contour tool from Spatial Analyst, then clipped to the delta polygon with the Clip tool from Analysis toolbox. To create continuous spatially aligned data across the mapping domain, interpolated values were extracted from the raster cells back to the spatially aligned points with the Extract Multi Values to Points tool. Each extracted value was assigned a unique CL ID according to its location.
